title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee March 17–18, 2026
summary: The FOMC held its March 17-18, 2026 meeting amid significant market turbulence driven by AI-related business model concerns and a Middle East conflict that caused crude oil front-month futures to surge approximately 50 percent. Market rate expectations shifted notably higher, with a rate cut not fully priced in until December and the probability of rate hikes through early next year rising to about 30 percent, though the Desk Survey still showed two 25bp cuts expected. The Committee noted elevated inflation pressures, with 12-month PCE inflation at 2.8 percent in January.
key_points: Crude oil front-month futures prices increased approximately 50 percent during the intermeeting period due to Middle East conflict
Rate cut not fully priced in until December; probability of rate hikes through early 2027 increased to approximately 30 percent
The Desk Survey median still showed two 25 basis point rate cuts expected this year, with respondents pushing out timing slightly
Broad equity prices fell about 5 percent over the period; software sector underperformed due to AI-related concerns
One-year inflation swap rate rose nearly 50 basis points, but forward measures beyond one year were little changed, suggesting transitory impact
Standing repo operations reached $30 billion on one day, the third-largest volume since inception, suggesting greater willingness to use operations following December changes
Reserves projected to average about $3 trillion through September after hitting late-April trough
Total PCE inflation was 2.8 percent in January 2026, with consumer price inflation remaining elevated
why_it_matters: These minutes capture the Fed's assessment during a period of elevated uncertainty driven by two distinct shocks: AI-related disruptions to business models and a Middle East conflict spiking energy prices. The divergence between market pricing (no cuts until December, ~30% chance of hikes) and the Desk Survey (two cuts expected) highlights uncertainty about the inflation versus growth tradeoff. Rising term premiums reflecting uncertainty, equity pressure from AI concerns, and private credit redemption increases signal potential financial stability considerations that could influence policy calculus.
macro_context: The Fed is navigating a complex macro backdrop with elevated energy prices threatening to reignite inflation while equity markets and growth face headwinds from AI disruptions. Near-term inflation expectations have risen notably, but longer-term measures remain anchored, suggesting the Committee may view the oil shock as transitory. Global central banks including the ECB, BoC, and SNB are now expected to hike rates modestly, complicating the Fed's decision space.
market_structure_context: Treasury market liquidity diminished modestly but functioned well. Standing repo operations saw increased usage ($30bn on highest settlement day), suggesting improved willingness to use these facilities following December framework changes. Money market conditions remained broadly stable with effective federal funds rate 1bp below IORB. After April, the pace of reserve management purchases is expected to decline significantly as swings in nonreserve liabilities moderate.
important_numbers: 50% — crude oil front-month futures price increase over intermeeting period
50 bps — one-year inflation swap rate rise over period
~30% — probability of rate hikes through early 2027 implied by options
~5% — broad equity price decline over intermeeting period
$30 billion — peak standing repo usage day (third highest ever)
$3 trillion — projected average reserves through September
1 bp — EFFR spread below IORB rate
notes: Source is a primary FOMC minutes document from the Federal Reserve, high domain trust tier. Document is recent (published March 18, 2026, within 90 days of TODAY). Content was truncated but provided sufficient detail on financial markets, policy rate expectations, and economic conditions. Key figures on oil prices, equity declines, rate probability shifts, and money market operations are explicitly stated in the source. Some economic data (like PCE) is referenced from earlier period due to truncation, but what was captured is reliable and directly quoted.
